summary:
The Pengtoushan culture is an early Neolithic culture of the middle Yangtze River region in Hunan Province, China, dated to approximately 7500–6100 BCE. The site of Pengtoushan, from which the culture takes its name, is one of the earliest known rice-cultivating communities in China and has yielded rice phytoliths, husks, and grains embedded in pottery and in storage contexts that provide direct evidence of rice agriculture at a very early stage.

Pengtoushan communities lived in small villages with rectangular timber-frame structures, sometimes elevated on stilts to manage the seasonal flooding characteristic of the middle Yangtze basin. In addition to rice, they practiced fishing and hunting and produced a distinctive pottery tradition. The Pengtoushan culture is an important early representative of the rice-farming tradition of the middle Yangtze that would develop into later, more complex cultures, ultimately contributing to the sophisticated Neolithic societies such as Daxi and the spectacular Liangzhu culture that flourished in the Yangtze delta. Together with the [[event:1270|Shangshan culture]] further east, Pengtoushan helps define the broad geographical extent and deep antiquity of [[event:6|Yangtze valley rice agriculture]].
